revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Gorintō]][[en:Gorintō]][[es:Gorintō]][[fa:گورینتو]][[fr:Gorintō]][[it:Gorintō]][[ja:五輪塔]][[ko:오륜탑]][[nl:Gorintō]][[zh:五轮塔]]
{{Short description|Japanese funerary pagoda}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[Image:Mimizuka-M1773 corrected.jpg|thumb|200px|right|A ''gorintō'' on top of the {{ill|Mimizuka|en|Mimizuka|qid=Q707668|lt=Mimizuka|1=Mimizuka}} with {{ill|Siddhaṃ script|en|Siddhaṃ script|qid=Q250379|lt=Siddhaṃ|1=Siddhaṃ script}} inscriptions]] {{nihongo|'''''Gorintō'''''|五輪塔|1=Gorintō|1=Gorintō}} ("five-ringed tower") is a Japanese type of {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} [[Japanese pagoda|pagoda]] believed to have been first adopted by the [[Shingon]] and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} sects during the mid {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref>{{cite web|last=JAANUS|title=Tou|url=http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/deta/t/tou.htm|access-date=3 July 2010}}</ref> It is used for memorial or funerary purposes<ref name="ko">Kōjien Japanese Dictionary</ref> and is therefore common in Buddhist temples and cemeteries. It is also called {{nihongo|''gorinsotōba or gorinsotoba''|五輪卒塔婆}} ("five-ringed stupa") or {{nihongo|''goringedatsu''|五輪解脱}},<ref name="ko"/><ref name="HC"/> where the term ''sotoba'' is a transliteration of the Sanskrit word [[stupa]].<ref>Article "Buttō", Japanese Wikipedia, accessed on April 10, 2008</ref> The stupa was originally a structure or other sacred building containing a relic of Buddha or of a saint,<ref>Article [[Stupa]] accessed on April 10, 2008</ref> then it was gradually stylized in various ways and its shape can change quite a bit according to the era and to the country where it is found.<ref name="HC">Home Study Course on Basic Buddhism</ref> Often offertory strips of wood with five subdivisions and covered with elaborate inscriptions also called ''sotoba'' can be found at tombs in Japanese cemeteries (see photo below).<ref name="ko"/> The inscriptions contain {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} texts and the posthumous name of the dead person. These can be considered stupa variants.
== Structure and meaning == [[File:Pagoda.svg|thumb|200px|left|Parts of a Gorintō]] In all its variations, the ''gorintō'' includes five rings (although that number can often be difficult to detect by decoration), each having one of the five shapes symbolic of the Five Elements, ([[:en:Mahabhuta|Mahabhuta]] in {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}, or [[:en:Godai|Godai]] in Japanese): the earth ring (cube), the water ring (sphere), the fire ring (pyramid), the air ring (crescent), and the ether ring, (or energy, or void).<ref name="HC"/> The last two rings (air and ether) are visually and conceptually united into a single subgroup. The last shape, ether, is the one that changes most according to the country,<ref name="HC"/> and in Japan it is close to that of a lotus flower.<ref name="HC"/> The rings express the idea that after death our physical bodies will go back to their original, elemental form.<ref name="HC"/> ''Gorintō'' are usually made of stone, but some built in wood, metal, or crystal exist.
[[Image:Ryosen-ji02.jpg|thumb|200px|right|''Gorintō'' bearing Lotus Sutra carvings]] On each section are often carved from top to bottom the Sanskrit letters ''kha'' (void, or {{nihongo|Kū|空}} in Japanese), ''ha'' (air, or {{nihongo|Fū|風}}), ''ra'' (fire, or {{nihongo|Ka|火}}), ''va'' (water, or {{nihongo|Sui|水}}), and ''a'' (earth, or {{nihongo|Chi|地}}),<ref name="JW">Article "Gorintō", Japanese Wikipedia, accessed on April 10, 2008</ref><ref>Article [[:en:Godai|Godai]], accessed on April 10, 2008</ref> and in {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478qid=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}} and {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} temples sometimes a ''gorintō'' will have carved on it the name of the {{nihongo|{{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}|妙法蓮華経|myōhō-renge-kyō}} (see photo).
The ''gorintō'' as a symbol belongs to {{nihongo|{{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}}|密教}} literally "secret teachings", term often translated as "esoteric Buddhism"), a {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} term that refers to the {{ill|Esotericism|en|Esotericism|qid=Q131505|lt=esoteric|1=Esotericism}} [[:en:Vajrayāna|Vajrayāna]] practices of the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} school and the related practices that make up part of the {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} school.<ref>Article [[:en:Mikkyo|Mikkyo]], accessed on April 10, 2008</ref> In those esoteric disciplines, the first two shapes (the cube and the sphere) represent the most perfect doctrine, and are supposed to contain in themselves the other three.<ref name="HC"/> Together they represent the {{nihongo|''Jitsuzaikai''|実在界}} (lit. "real world"), that is the realm of perfect understanding, whereas the others constitute the {{nihongo|''Henkai''|変界}} (lit. "world of mutation")，or the world of impermanence, which includes the {{nihongo|genshōkai|現象界}}, the world we live in.<ref name="HC"/>
On a second level of symbolism, each part of the ''gorintō'' also represents an element of change in both ''Jitsuzaikai'' and ''Henkai''.<ref name="HC"/>
* The top symbol fuses the shape of a crescent, representing wisdom, and a triangle, representing principle.<ref name="HC"/> The fusion of these qualities in the Genshōkai and Henkai represents perfection, or Buddhahood.<ref name="HC"/> * The crescent of water represents receptivity,<ref name="HC"/> and is similar to a cup ready to receive from the heavens.<ref name="HC"/> * The triangle is a point of transition between two worlds and as such represents both unification and movement.<ref name="HC"/> It indicates the activity necessary to reach Enlightenment.<ref name="HC"/> * The circle represents completion, or Wisdom attainment.<ref name="HC"/> * The square is a symbol of the four elements.<ref name="HC"/>
[[File:Sotoba At Kurosawa-Akira's-grave.jpg|thumb|Some wooden ''sotoba'' at a tomb. Note the division in five sections]]On a last level of symbolism, the shapes represent the order in which the student progresses in his or her spiritual studies.<ref name="HC"/>
* The square is the basis, the will to attain perfection.<ref name="HC"/> * The circle is the attainment of equanimity.<ref name="HC"/> * The triangle represents the energy created in pursuit of the truth.<ref name="HC"/> * The crescent represents the development of intuition and awareness.<ref name="HC"/> * The topmost shape represents perfection.<ref name="HC"/>
== History in Japan ==
The theory of five elements was born in India, but the development of the Japanese ''gorintō'' shows the deep influence of Mikkyō, and in particular of {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} and {{ill|Kakuban|en|Kakuban|qid=Q1676043|lt=Kakuban|1=Kakuban}}.<ref name="JW"/> The use of ''gorintō'' is thought to have begun in the second half of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.<ref name="JW"/> The oldest known examples can be found at Chūsonji, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}, are a mix of ''gorintō'' and ''[[tahōtō|hōtō]]'' (two-storied Buddhist tower) and go back to 1169.<ref name="JW"/> They then came into normal use during the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} and are still widely used today for memorial monuments and tombs, particularly but not exclusively in Buddhist temples. A ''gorintō'' is for example the traditional headstone shape of a [[Shingon]] sect grave.<ref name="HC"/>
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== References == {{Commons Category|Gorintō}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20020221180145/http://geocities.com/gorinto/sotoba_expl.html The Sotoba (Gorintō, Stupa) Explained], Home Study Course on Basic Buddhism accessed on April 6, 2008 * Iwanami {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}}|広辞苑}} Japanese dictionary, 6th Edition (2008), DVD version {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}
